verses.text: Then all Israel gathered themselves to David unto Hebron, saying, Behold, we are thy bone and thy flesh. And moreover in time past, even when Saul was king, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel: and the LORD thy God said unto thee, Thou shalt feed my people Israel, and thou shalt be ruler over my people Israel. Therefore came all the elders of Israel to the king to Hebron; and David made a covenant with them in Hebron before the LORD; and they anointed David king over Israel, according to the word of the LORD by Samuel. Guidance: At last the long-delayed promise is fulfilled: all Israel gathers to David at Hebron and anoints him king — and the Chronicler is careful to say it was done 'according to the word of the LORD by Samuel.' Years before, Samuel had anointed the shepherd boy in secret; now the whole nation publicly confirms what God had long ago decreed. The chapter is showing us the LORD's faithfulness to bring His purposes to pass in His own time; David did not seize the throne but waited through long years of rejection and exile until the LORD gave it to him through the willing consent of all Israel. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's promises are sure, though their fulfillment may be long delayed — what God had spoken by Samuel came true at Hebron. The chapter is teaching us, too, the right pattern of godly rule: David 'made a covenant with them in Hebron before the LORD,' binding king and people together under God; and the people remembered the LORD's charge that David should 'feed my people Israel' — kingship as shepherding, not lording. The chapter is teaching us that true authority among God's people is a stewardship to feed and serve, exercised before the LORD and answerable to Him. And in the people's words 'we are thy bone and thy flesh,' we glimpse the union of the king with his people — a foreshadowing of Christ, great David's greater Son, who took our flesh and blood to be our King and Shepherd, anointed according to the eternal word of God. The kingdom that begins here at Hebron points forward to the everlasting kingdom of the LORD's true Anointed.

And David and all Israel went to Jerusalem, which is Jebus; where the Jebusites were, the inhabitants of the land. And the inhabitants of Jebus said to David, Thou shalt not come hither. Nevertheless David took the castle of Zion, which is the city of David. And David said, Whosoever smiteth the Jebusites first shall be chief and captain. So Joab the son of Zeruiah went first up, and was chief. And David dwelt in the castle; therefore they called it the city of David. And he built the city round about, even from Millo round about: and Joab repaired the rest of the city. So David waxed greater and greater: for the LORD of hosts was with him. Guidance: One of David's first acts as king is to take the stronghold of Jebus — the fortress the tribes had never managed to capture — and make it his capital, Jerusalem, the city of David, the place where the temple would one day stand. The Jebusites boasted 'Thou shalt not come hither,' but their proud defiance fell before David: 'Nevertheless David took the castle of Zion.' And the Chronicler gives the secret of it all in a single sentence: 'So David waxed greater and greater: for the LORD of hosts was with him.' The chapter is showing us that David's rising power flowed not from his own strength or strategy but from the presence of God; the LORD of hosts was with him, and therefore no fortress could stand against him. The chapter is teaching us that the true source of all success in the LORD's service is His presence — 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' The proud walls of Jebus, deemed impregnable, were no match for a man with whom God dwelt. The chapter is teaching us not to be daunted by the boasts of our enemies or the strength of obstacles that have long resisted us; what others could not do, David did, because the LORD was with him. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD chose Zion to be His city and David's, the place He would set His name — and that He prospered His servant to establish it. The God who made David 'greater and greater' is the same God who advances His purposes through those who walk with Him. Let us seek above all that the LORD of hosts be with us, for that presence is greater than every wall and stronger than every foe.

These also are the chief of the mighty men whom David had, who strengthened themselves with him in his kingdom, and with all Israel, to make him king, according to the word of the LORD concerning Israel. And this is the number of the mighty men whom David had; Jashobeam, an Hachmonite, the chief of the captains: he lifted up his spear against three hundred slain by him at one time. And after him was Eleazar the son of Dodo, the Ahohite, who was one of the three mighties. He was with David at Pasdammim, and there the Philistines were gathered together to battle, where was a parcel of ground full of barley; and the people fled from before the Philistines. And they set themselves in the midst of that parcel, and delivered it, and slew the Philistines; and the LORD saved them by a great deliverance. Guidance: The Chronicler now honours the mighty men who rallied to David — the chief captains 'who strengthened themselves with him in his kingdom... to make him king, according to the word of the LORD.' We read of Jashobeam, who struck down three hundred at one time, and of Eleazar, who with David stood his ground in a field of barley when all the people fled, and held it against the Philistines. And again the Chronicler points past the human valour to its true cause: 'the LORD saved them by a great deliverance.' The chapter is showing us the LORD raising up valiant men around His chosen king — strong, brave, loyal men who gave their strength to establish David's kingdom. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's anointed does not stand alone; God surrounds His servants with helpers, knitting faithful hearts to the cause He is advancing. The chapter is teaching us the honour of standing firm when others flee: Eleazar held the barley field when 'the people fled,' and his courage became the occasion of a great victory. There is a holy stubbornness in the faithful — to hold the ground God has given when others give way. Yet the chapter is teaching us, above all, that even the mightiest deeds of the bravest men are the LORD's doing: 'the LORD saved them by a great deliverance.' The strength of Jashobeam and the courage of Eleazar were real, but the victory was God's. The chapter is teaching us to be valiant and steadfast in our place, and yet to ascribe every triumph to the LORD who works through His servants and saves His people by great deliverances.

Now three of the thirty captains went down to the rock to David, into the cave of Adullam; and the host of the Philistines encamped in the valley of Rephaim. And David was then in the hold, and the Philistines' garrison was then at Bethlehem. And David longed, and said, Oh that one would give me drink of the water of the well of Bethlehem, that is at the gate! And the three brake through the host of the Philistines, and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem, that was by the gate, and took it, and brought it to David: but David would not drink of it, but poured it out to the LORD, And said, My God forbid it me, that I should do this thing: shall I drink the blood of these men that have put their lives in jeopardy? for with the jeopardy of their lives they brought it. Therefore he would not drink it. These things did these three mightiest. Guidance: Here is one of the most beautiful scenes in all of David's story. Worn and thirsty in the hold, David sighed aloud for a drink from the well of Bethlehem, his home town — now held by the Philistines. Three of his mighty men, hearing his longing, broke through the enemy host at the risk of their lives, drew the water, and brought it to him. And David would not drink it. Instead he 'poured it out to the LORD,' saying it would be as drinking the very blood of the men who had jeopardized their lives to bring it. The chapter is showing us a king of rare nobility and tenderness — David so honoured the love and courage of his men that he could not treat their costly gift as a mere indulgence of his own thirst; he lifted it up to God as something too sacred for himself. The chapter is teaching us the preciousness of lives laid down in love, and the reverence with which such sacrifice should be received; David counted the water holy because it had been bought with such devotion. The chapter is teaching us self-denial and the refusal to consume for our own comfort what has cost others dearly. And in pouring the water out as an offering 'to the LORD,' David teaches us that the truest response to costly love and devotion is worship — to give the best back to God. And Abishai the brother of Joab, he was chief of the three: for lifting up his spear against three hundred, he slew them, and had a name among the three. Of the three, he was more honourable than the two; for he was their captain: howbeit he attained not to the first three. Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, the son of a valiant man of Kabzeel, who had done many acts; he slew two lionlike men of Moab: also he went down and slew a lion in a pit in a snowy day. And he slew an Egyptian, a man of great stature, five cubits high; and in the Egyptian's hand was a spear like a weaver's beam; and he went down to him with a staff, and plucked the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and slew him with his own spear. These things did Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and had the name among the three mighties. Behold, he was honourable among the thirty, but attained not to the first three: and David set him over his guard. Guidance: The Chronicler records the exploits of Abishai and Benaiah — men of extraordinary courage. Benaiah's deeds are especially vivid: he slew two champions of Moab, went down into a pit and killed a lion 'in a snowy day,' and faced a giant Egyptian armed only with a staff, wrenched the spear from his hand, and killed him with his own weapon. For these things he was set over David's bodyguard. The chapter is showing us the valour the LORD raised up to serve His king — men who faced lions and giants and did not flinch. The chapter is teaching us that genuine courage acts even in the worst circumstances: Benaiah went down into the pit against the lion 'in a snowy day,' in the cold and the slippery danger, when every reason counseled retreat. The faithful do not wait for ideal conditions to do their duty. The chapter is teaching us, too, a quiet lesson in the ranking of these men — Abishai and Benaiah were mighty 'howbeit attained not to the first three.' There is an order of honour even among the valiant, and each man is recorded in his place without envy. The chapter is teaching us to do great things faithfully in whatever rank the LORD assigns us, content to serve well whether we are among the first three or among the thirty. Benaiah's reward for his courage was greater responsibility — David set him over the guard — for faithfulness in valour is rewarded with trust. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD takes note of brave and faithful service, and that those who acquit themselves well in hard places are fitted by Him for positions of trust in His kingdom.

Also the valiant men of the armies were, Asahel the brother of Joab, Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem, Shammoth the Harorite, Helez the Pelonite, Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, Abiezer the Antothite, Sibbecai the Hushathite, Ilai the Ahohite, Maharai the Netophathite, Heled the son of Baanah the Netophathite, Ithai the son of Ribai of Gibeah, that pertained to the children of Benjamin, Benaiah the Pirathonite, Hurai of the brooks of Gaash, Abiel the Arbathite, Azmaveth the Baharumite, Eliahba the Shaalbonite, The sons of Hashem the Gizonite, Jonathan the son of Shage the Hararite, Ahiam the son of Sacar the Hararite, Eliphal the son of Ur, Hepher the Mecherathite, Ahijah the Pelonite, Hezro the Carmelite, Naarai the son of Ezbai, Joel the brother of Nathan, Mibhar the son of Haggeri, Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai the Berothite, the armourbearer of Joab the son of Zeruiah, Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite, Uriah the Hittite, Zabad the son of Ahlai, Adina the son of Shiza the Reubenite, a captain of the Reubenites, and thirty with him, Hanan the son of Maachah, and Joshaphat the Mithnite, Uzzia the Ashterathite, Shama and Jehiel the sons of Hothan the Aroerite, Jediael the son of Shimri, and Joha his brother, the Tizite, Eliel the Mahavite, and Jeribai, and Joshaviah, the sons of Elnaam, and Ithmah the Moabite, Eliel, and Obed, and Jasiel the Mesobaite. Guidance: The Chronicler sets down the long roll of David's valiant men — name after name, from many towns and even many nations (an Ammonite, a Moabite, and others among them). Each is recorded individually, his name and origin preserved forever in the sacred record. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's king was served by a great company of faithful men, and that not one of them was forgotten; each name is written down with care. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD remembers those who give themselves to His cause; the humblest warrior in David's host has his name in Scripture as surely as the chief of the three. There is honour with God for all who serve faithfully, however little known. The chapter is teaching us, in the presence of foreigners among the names — an Ammonite, a Moabite, a Hittite — that David's kingdom drew in men from the nations, a quiet foreshadowing of the kingdom of David's Son, who gathers His servants from every people and tribe. And one name in the list arrests the heart: 'Uriah the Hittite.' Here is the loyal, valiant foreigner whom David would later betray, sending him to his death to cover his sin with Bathsheba. The Chronicler records Uriah's name among the faithful mighty men — a silent, sober witness to the worth of the man David wronged. The chapter is teaching us, even in a list of honour, that David was a man and a sinner; that the same king capable of pouring out water rather than waste his men's devotion would one day pour out the life of a loyal man to serve his own lust. The roll of the mighty men thus stands both as a tribute to faithful service and, in Uriah's name, as a reminder that only One King is without sin — the Son of David, who is faithful to all who serve Him and betrays none.
